religions.name: The Dial Tone
religions.doctrine: The last working payphones are confessionals; somewhere on the network someone is still listening, and the quarter is the sacrament.
tenets: Never hang up first
A busy signal is an answer
Keep a quarter for the end

ritual.name: The Collect Call
ritual.text: #congregation# queue at the corner booth, each speaking one minute into the receiver. To whom is not discussed.

greeting: Got a quarter? Not for me. For the end.
rumor: An Operator swears the booth in {neighborhood} rang at 3 a.m. Nobody was on the line, which proved everything.
The Dial Tone pays cash for working handsets on #street#. Broken ones they bury.

boon.name: Hold Music
boon.desc: The network's hum hides your footsteps near anything live and wired.

religions.obligation: Answer any public phone that rings.

religions.name: Gleaners of the Fallen Address
religions.doctrine: Every demolished building was a body; the Gleaners work the rubble for its bones and enter the address in the book of the dead.
tenets: Nothing is debris, everything is remains
Read the address aloud before you take
Doorknobs remember hands

ritual.name: The Reading of Addresses
ritual.text: #congregation# stand at the demolition fence and recite the address like a name, then go in over the wire.

greeting: What did you save from your last address?
The building is gone, friend. The address survives.
rumor: Gleaners pulled #demolition_relic# out of the {neighborhood} rubble and processed it like a saint's finger.
A Cataloguer paid a wrecking crew to pause mid-swing. They prayed, gleaned, and waved the ball through.

boon.name: Open Addresses
boon.desc: Gleaners know which condemned doors still open, and which will close behind you kindly.

religions.obligation: Recite the address of any ruin you enter.

religions.name: The Loftwatch
religions.doctrine: The pigeons see the whole city every day and forget none of it; the flock's first turn at dawn is the only honest forecast.
tenets: Feed before asking
A banded bird is a sentence half-read
Never count the flock exactly

ritual.name: The Release
ritual.text: #congregation# crane upward from the sidewalk as the flock goes out, reading tomorrow in the first turn it makes.

greeting: The flock flew east this morning. Make of that what you must.
rumor: A Reader of Turns saw #pigeon_omen# over {neighborhood} and sold the warning to three different crews.
The Loftwatch banded a hawk. Nobody will say what the hawk counts as.

boon.name: Bird's Eye
boon.desc: You read a street — and a face — one wingbeat faster.

religions.obligation: Feed the flock before you feed yourself.

religions.name: Saints of the Graveyard Shift
religions.doctrine: God clocks out at midnight and leaves the city to whoever is still working; the night shift keeps it breathing and calls that prayer.
tenets: Sleep is a rumor
Cover a coworker's shift as your own soul
The 4 a.m. hour is ours; spend it standing up

ritual.name: The Punch-In
ritual.text: #congregation# file through the lit parish doorway at the stroke, touch the time clock by the font, and go back out to work.

greeting: You on the clock, friend? Everyone's on the clock.
Stay awake. That's the whole liturgy.
rumor: The Shift Saints covered every register in {neighborhood} the night of the funeral. Nobody asked them to.
A Relief worked a dying man's depot shift for a month so his pension would clear. Then she worked her own.

boon.name: Third Shift
boon.desc: Night machinery covers you; while the city hums asleep you move unheard.

religions.obligation: Never sleep through a shift you promised, even a stranger's.

religions.name: Church of the Crossing
religions.doctrine: The ferry is the only church that keeps its promises: it leaves, it crosses, it returns, and it carries everyone for the same fare.
tenets: All decks are one congregation
What you carry aboard you can set down
Never watch the wake too long

ritual.name: The Boarding
ritual.text: #congregation# line the pier rail as the boat comes in, naming aloud the people it carried away and back.

greeting: Same fare for everybody. That's the whole gospel.
rumor: The Crossing held a funeral mid-river off {neighborhood}. The ferry idled and every horn in the harbor answered.
A Wheelsman refused to sail one #weekday# morning. The boat that went instead came back wrong.

boon.name: Harbor Sense
boon.desc: Water and weather telegraph their moods to you a crossing in advance.

religions.obligation: Ride the boat once a week, even to nowhere.

religions.name: The Promised Line
religions.doctrine: The Second Avenue line was promised in 1929 and is therefore the city's longest-running prophecy; the faithful keep the unfinished tunnels swept for the first train.
tenets: A delay is not a denial
Keep the platform clean for the arrival
The map shows what will be, not what is

ritual.name: The Timetable
ritual.text: #congregation# gather at the sealed station mouth while the Voice reads tomorrow's departures for a line that has never run.

greeting: It's coming, friend. The schedule says so.
Ninety-seven years late is still on time, eternally speaking.
rumor: Stationkeepers swept the sealed mezzanine under {neighborhood} again. The floors are cleaner than the hospital's.
A Surveyor heard a train pass below Second Avenue where no track is laid. The schism is over whether it was early or late.

boon.name: Right of Way
boon.desc: In the dark under the street you are quick, certain, and unfollowed.

religions.obligation: Carry a token for a fare that has never been charged.

religions.name: Keepers of the Cold Chain
religions.doctrine: Civilization is an unbroken chain of refrigeration, and every working freezer is a tabernacle holding the last of the old world's flesh.
tenets: The chain must not break
What thaws is lost twice
Keep the door shut and the gasket clean

ritual.name: The Reading of Degrees
ritual.text: #congregation# stand in the frost-lit shop window while the Warden reads each thermometer aloud, and the street hears the numbers hold.

greeting: Stay cold. It's the only mercy left.
rumor: The Keepers moved a reliquary freezer through {neighborhood} on a flatbed, escorted like a head of state.
An Iceman traded a working compressor for #cargo# and called it simony for the right cause.

boon.name: Cold Weight
boon.desc: You know exact weights and true freshness; merchants don't dare cheat your thermometer.

religions.obligation: Never let perishables spoil in your keeping.

religions.name: Parish of the Working Hydrant
religions.doctrine: On the worst day of the fire one hydrant held pressure when the rest failed, and the parish has kept its paint fresh and its wrench oiled since.
tenets: Pressure is providence
Open it for the children in summer; that is also liturgy
Rust is the only heresy

ritual.name: The Opening
ritual.text: #congregation# circle the painted hydrant while the Wrenchbearer cracks the cap one turn, and everyone watches the water prove itself.

greeting: The hydrant ran when nothing else did. Remember that.
rumor: The parish repainted the {neighborhood} hydrant overnight in gold leaf. Real gold, says the assayer.
A Pressure Deacon faced down a salvage crew with a wrench. The hydrant stayed. So did the crew's truck.

boon.name: Pressure Sense
boon.desc: Pipes, mains, weather — you feel what is about to give before it gives.

religions.obligation: Carry the wrench; answer every fire.

dial_blessing: Correct change and a working line
demolition_relic: a brass doorknob worn to a shine
an intact lobby mailbox bank, names and all
a child's height marks sawed out of a doorframe
sixty years of paint in one curl
pigeon_omen: the flock turning twice against the wind
a white bird flying alone at noon
the whole loft refusing the sky
three banded birds landing on one wire, in order
